_meta.purpose: Machine-readable knowledge pack for AWS Strands as a conversion SOURCE. Keys describe native Strands constructs and how each maps to the neutral IR when parsed. Parsing lives in AWSStrandsSourceAdapter (adapters/source/aws_strands_adapter.py): Agent(tools=[...]) -> one SINGLE_AGENT node; @tool fns -> node tools; model ctor -> node config; Swarm/Graph/workflow -> multiple agent nodes. This file exposes the framework to the UI and grounds Tier 3. No Strands target generator exists yet.

Agent.notes: The one central primitive: a model-driven tool-calling loop. Callable directly (agent('prompt')). A lone Agent(tools=[...]) is ONE node, never a multi-node graph. Default model provider is Amazon Bedrock.

agentic_loop.notes: Model decides control flow: call model -> optional tool-use requests -> Strands runs tools -> feed results back -> repeat until final answer. No author-declared edges. Collapses to the single node in the IR.

Swarm.ir_equivalent: multiple agent nodes + handoff orchestration notes
Swarm.notes: Self-organising handoff between agents via a shared handoff tool.

Graph.pattern: b = GraphBuilder(); b.add_node(agent, 'id'); b.add_edge('a', 'b'); b.set_entry_point('a'); graph = b.build()
Graph.notes: The only author-declared graph in Strands, and it is a graph of AGENTS, not of low-level steps. Edges may carry conditions.

workflow.notes: Sequences agent tasks with dependencies. Another multi-agent orchestration form.

agent_state.notes: Key/value scratch store NOT part of the model conversation. There is NO global mutable graph-state dict like LangGraph; do not model it as reducer fields on a shared state model.

request_state.notes: Per-invocation state available to tools/hooks; scoped to a single agent call.

conversation_manager.import: from strands.agent.conversation_manager import SlidingWindowConversationManager, SummarizingConversationManager, NullConversationManager
conversation_manager.notes: Controls context-window trimming. SlidingWindow keeps last N messages; Summarizing summarises older turns; Null disables trimming.

single_agent.notes: The entire core agent, including its full agentic loop and all its tools, is exactly one node. Tools are attached to the node, not modelled as separate nodes.

edges.notes: N/A for a single agent: the model drives control flow inside the agentic loop, so there are NO author-declared edges. Explicit edges exist ONLY in multi-agent orchestration.

multi_agent_orchestration.swarm_handoff: Agents hand off via a shared handoff tool (Swarm) -> orchestration notes between agent nodes.
multi_agent_orchestration.graph_edge: GraphBuilder.add_edge('a', 'b'), optionally conditional -> edges between agent nodes.
multi_agent_orchestration.workflow_dependency: workflow task dependencies -> task-dependency notes between agent nodes.

@tool.ir_equivalent: a tool on the agent node
@tool.registration: Pass the function object in Agent(tools=[...]).
@tool.pattern:
@tool
def get_history(test_id: str, path: str | None = None) -> dict:
    """Return CI run stats for one test.

    Args:
        test_id: The unique test identifier.
        path: Optional CI-history JSON file path.
    """
    ...
@tool.notes: Strands introspects type hints + docstring (incl. Args:) to build the tool schema. Do not model each tool as its own node.

prebuilt_tools.import: from strands_tools import calculator, file_read, http_request
prebuilt_tools.notes: Import from strands_tools (underscore), NOT from strands. Some (shell, python_repl) execute arbitrary code.

module_tools.notes: A Python module exposing TOOL_SPEC + matching function can be passed directly (module object or dotted path) without the @tool decorator.

mcp_tools.pattern:
with MCPClient(...) as mcp:
    agent = Agent(tools=mcp.list_tools_sync())
mcp_tools.notes: Consume tools from an MCP server. Use the client as a context manager; each MCP tool becomes a tool on the node.

agent_as_tool.ir_equivalent: tool edge / delegation note between agent nodes
agent_as_tool.notes: Hierarchical composition: wrap a specialist agent inside a @tool given to an orchestrator agent.

hitl.reality: Strands has NO native interrupt/resume primitive (unlike LangGraph interrupt() or MAF RequestInfoExecutor). The agentic loop does not pause itself for a human, and there is no built-in checkpoint-and-await-human mechanism.

workaround_human_input_tool.notes: Model calls this tool when it needs a human; the return value flows back into the loop. strands_tools also ships handoff_to_user. Parses as just another tool on the node, flagged as HITL.

workaround_loop_break.notes: Stop the agent where a human must act, do the human step in host code, then start a new invocation (optionally with a session_manager so the conversation persists across the pause).

hitl.anti_pattern: Do NOT emit strands.interrupt(...) or claim Strands natively pauses/resumes. HITL is a human-input tool OR an explicit loop break, and must be described honestly.

session_managers.notes: Persist the whole agent session (messages + state) so a conversation survives restarts. This is durability, not a pause/resume-for-HITL checkpoint like MAF's.

callable_sync.notes: Most common form. Agent instances are callable.

stream_async.notes: Async generator of incremental events (text deltas in event['data'], tool-use events, lifecycle markers).

structured_output.notes: Runs the loop and returns a validated instance of the Pydantic model.

providers.notes: Strands is provider-pluggable via strands.models. The model is injected; swap the constructor to change provider. DEFAULT is Amazon Bedrock (a bare Agent() uses Bedrock and needs AWS credentials).

reject_list.description: Any Strands parsing/output containing the following is an error.
items: claim that Strands natively pauses/resumes for HITL
from strands import StateGraph  (LangGraph, not Strands)
from strands import ChatAgent  (MAF, not Strands)
from strands import calculator  (prebuilt tools live in strands_tools)
modelling each @tool of one agent as its own IR node
assuming OpenAI is the default provider  (default is Bedrock)
treating a lone Agent(tools=[...]) as a multi-node graph  (it is one SINGLE_AGENT node)
stdlib module in requirements.txt (json, os, io, re, math, hashlib, uuid, typing)
